---
title: Quarto presentation
author: Amelia Urquhart
image: images/pres.png
css: styles.css
filters:
  - ../../date.lua
---
---
title: "Cells: Structure, Function, and Significance"
format: 
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    toc: true
---

## What Are Cells?

Cells are the **basic structural and functional units of life**.  
All living organisms—unicellular or multicellular—are composed of cells.

> “The cell is the fundamental unit of life.”  
> — *Tortora & Derrickson, Principles of Anatomy & Physiology*

---

## Cell Theory

### Developed from work by Schleiden, Schwann, and Virchow

- All living things are composed of cells  
- Cells are the basic units of structure and function  
- All cells arise from pre‑existing cells  

**Source:** *Mader, Human Biology; Alberts et al., Molecular Biology of the Cell*

---

## Two Major Cell Types

### Prokaryotic Cells
- No nucleus  
- No membrane‑bound organelles  
- Smaller and simpler  
- Example: bacteria  

### Eukaryotic Cells
- Have a nucleus  
- Contain membrane‑bound organelles  
- Found in plants, animals, fungi, protists  

**Source:** *Campbell Biology, 12th ed.*

---

## The Plasma Membrane

- Selectively permeable barrier  
- Composed of a **phospholipid bilayer** with embedded proteins  
- Maintains homeostasis  
- Responsible for transport, communication, and structural integrity  

**Source:** *Tortora & Derrickson, Principles of Anatomy & Physiology*

---

## Cytoplasm

- Region between the plasma membrane and nucleus  
- Contains:
  - **Cytosol** (fluid)
  - **Organelles** (functional structures)

**Source:** *Marieb & Hoehn, Human Anatomy & Physiology*

---

## The Nucleus

- Control center of the cell  
- Contains DNA organized into chromosomes  
- Surrounded by nuclear envelope  
- Site of transcription (DNA → RNA)

**Source:** *Alberts et al., Molecular Biology of the Cell*

---

## Major Organelles

### Mitochondria
- Produce ATP through cellular respiration  
- Contain their own DNA  

### Endoplasmic Reticulum
- Rough ER: protein synthesis  
- Smooth ER: lipid synthesis, detoxification  

### Golgi Apparatus
- Modifies, sorts, and packages proteins  

### Lysosomes
- Digestive enzymes for waste removal  

**Source:** *Marieb & Hoehn; Alberts et al.*

---

## The Cytoskeleton

- Network of protein filaments  
- Provides structure, movement, and transport  
- Components:
  - Microfilaments  
  - Intermediate filaments  
  - Microtubules  

**Source:** *Lodish et al., Molecular Cell Biology*

---

## Cell Transport Mechanisms

### Passive Transport
- No energy required  
- Diffusion, osmosis, facilitated diffusion  

### Active Transport
- Requires ATP  
- Moves substances against concentration gradient  

**Source:** *Tortora & Derrickson*

---

## Cell Communication

- Cells communicate via:
  - Chemical signals (hormones, neurotransmitters)  
  - Receptors on plasma membrane  
  - Signal transduction pathways  

**Source:** *Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology*

---

## Cell Division

### Mitosis
- Produces two identical daughter cells  
- Used for growth and repair  

### Meiosis
- Produces gametes  
- Reduces chromosome number by half  

**Source:** *Campbell Biology*

---

## Why Cells Matter in Anatomy & Physiology

- All tissues and organs are built from cells  
- Cellular dysfunction leads to disease  
  - Example: mitochondrial disorders  
  - Example: cancer (uncontrolled cell division)

**Source:** *Journal of Cell Biology; New England Journal of Medicine*

---

## References

- Alberts, B. *Molecular Biology of the Cell*. Garland Science.  
- Marieb, E., & Hoehn, K. *Human Anatomy & Physiology*. Pearson.  
- Tortora, G., & Derrickson, B. *Principles of Anatomy & Physiology*. Wiley.  
- Campbell, N. *Campbell Biology*. Pearson.  
- Lodish, H. *Molecular Cell Biology*. W.H. Freeman.  
- *Journal of Cell Biology*, Rockefeller University Press.  
- *Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology*, Springer Nature
